metadata.title: Vibecoding.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged

beats.narration_text: this is Liam, in for Bear. The practical position is blunt: vibecoding will not make you rich, and get-rich-quick Claude Code claims are mostly fiction. But vibecoding is still worth your time — for two specific, honest reasons. This is the one Claude Code guide for people who don't code.

props.command: what is vibecoding actually good for — and what is it not good for?

beats.narration_text: The definition: vibecoding is when you build a website or app by describing what you want in plain English and letting Claude write all the actual code. You don't write a single line. You vibe your way to a thing that runs. Claude Code is the specific tool for this — it lives in the Code tab of the Claude desktop app. The word 'vibe' is accurate in both the good and the bad sense. You get a running thing faster than you'd expect. The edges are rough. And that's fine, depending on what you're building it for.

artifactLines: You describe what you want in plain English. Claude writes all the code.
Claude Code lives in the Code tab of the Claude desktop app.
You won't write a single line of code. You will review every output.
Rough edges are fine — depends entirely on who sees the result.

beats.narration_text: There are exactly two good reasons to vibecode. One: you build a clickable version of your idea so that developers and designers stop guessing what you meant. A rough prototype beats a twelve-page requirements document. Send a working website to the dev team and they understand your vision in two minutes. Two: you build a small tool that does one specific thing better than a generic Claude chat interface — just for you, your team, or a handful of customers. If nobody outside that small group sees the rough edges, the rough edges don't matter.

artifactLines: Use case 1: build a clickable mockup to brief devs/designers — beats any spec doc.
Use case 2: build a small custom tool for you or a tiny team — rough edges are fine.
Not good for: public products, production software, things real users will break.
The honest filter: who sees the rough edges? If nobody, vibecode away.

beats.narration_text: Seven steps to set up Claude Code. One: get the app at claude.com/download, install it, open the Code tab. Two: create a clean folder on your computer, just for this project — nothing private, nothing you'd hate to lose, because Claude will read everything inside it. Three: turn on bypass permissions — without it, Claude asks your approval for every file operation, which slows everything down. Four: point Claude to your folder. Five: describe what you want. Six: review what it builds. Seven: iterate by talking to it, not by editing code manually.

shot.slotNote: SLOT — fill media/B03.png (screenshot of the Claude app Code tab with bypass permissions turned on and a clean folder pointed at it)

beats.narration_text: The first use case is a mockup for a consulting team to hand to developers. A non-coder can replace an ambiguous verbal description with a rough working website link. The developers see the layout, the flow, the feel. The designer gets the vision. This is not rocket science — it's a communication tool. The value is not in the software quality; it's in eliminating misunderstanding before expensive development starts.

shot.slotNote: SLOT — fill media/B04.png (screenshot of a vibecoded website mockup built with Claude Code — ideally a real example showing a working prototype)

beats.narration_text: The second use case is a custom LinkedIn analytics dashboard. Connect public post data through Apify, calculate an engagement score with your own weighting of likes, comments, and reposts, link every result to the original post, and add topic search. The value is specificity: Claude Code can build the small internal tool that no generic product provides. That's the ceiling of vibecoding: a small, personal, useful thing that nobody else makes exactly this way.

artifactLines: Connected Apify's public LinkedIn API to a simple dashboard.
Scores each post with a user-defined formula and weights for likes, comments, and reposts.
Clickable post links. Search by topic. Used daily.
A specific internal tool that generic products do not provide.
props.sparkLine: One tool, built for one person.

beats.narration_text: Now the honest limits. Vibecoding fails when the tool needs to be reliable for users who aren't you. Claude Code builds working things; it doesn't build tested, hardened, secure things. It will make mistakes in logic, skip edge cases, and produce code you can't read or debug if something breaks. It's not a substitute for a developer. It's a substitute for a static mockup or a very specific internal spreadsheet. The moment you're building something a customer will pay for, you need a real engineer involved — even if Claude wrote the first draft.

artifactLines: Not hardened: edge cases get missed, logic errors happen, no testing.
Not secure: Claude Code won't catch data leaks or auth problems.
Not debuggable by you: if it breaks, you can't read the code to fix it.
Rule: the moment a customer pays for it, get a real engineer involved.

beats.narration_text: A strong starting prompt pattern for Claude Code. Not 'build me a thing.' Too vague. The pattern that works: describe the one specific job the tool needs to do, who uses it, what inputs it takes, and what output it should produce. Then let Claude ask clarifying questions before it writes any code — because the questions Claude asks will tell you what you hadn't thought through yet. The first session is always more planning than building. That's correct. Changing the plan in plain English costs nothing. Changing the code later costs time.

props.title: vibecode-start-prompt.md
props.code:
Build me a [type of tool] that does [one specific job]. Who uses it: [me / my team of N / internal only]
Inputs: [what data or files it takes in]
Outputs: [what it produces or shows] Before writing any code, list your top 5 assumptions
and ask me any clarifying questions.
Then build it step by step.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Take the specific thing you'd build first — a mockup, a small dashboard, a one-purpose tool — and paste this into Claude Code to start.

props.command: I want to vibecode a small tool for personal use. It should [describe your one-sentence use case]. Before writing code: ask me 5 clarifying questions about inputs, outputs, and who uses it. Then build it step by step in my clean project folder.

beats.narration_text: Vibecoding won't make you rich. A working mockup and one custom tool that solves your actual problem — that's worth the hour. Nik Bear Brown.

props.title: Vibecoding.
props.subline: Mockups and small tools — nothing more
